confirmed.title: Substitution detector collapses every match in a calendar day into 2-3 round buckets and is keyed on round_date, not gaming_session_id
confirmed.evidence: _get_player_activity (lines 164-175) and _get_round_rosters (lines 202-230) query `... WHERE round_date LIKE $1` with `f"{session_date}%"` and then key rosters/activity by `round_number`. I confirmed via DB that player_comprehensive_stats.round_number only ever holds {0,1,2} (0:5615, 1:5910, 2:5851 rows) — it is the half-of-match index, NOT a per-session round counter. So `round_rosters` has at most 3 keys for an ENTIRE calendar day. _detect_roster_changes (lines 240-275) then diffs roster[round 1] vs roster[round 2], merging all matches of the day into one comparison. DB also shows multiple gaming_session_id values share a single round_date (e.g. 2026-03-25 = 4 sessions/75 rounds, 2026-06-16 = 2 sessions/26 rounds), so even the date scoping mixes distinct evenings. Output (late_joiners/early_leavers/substitutions) is therefore fabricated for any normal multi-match day. Violates the project rule 'ALWAYS use gaming_session_id for session queries (NOT dates)'. Mitigating: grep shows SubstitutionDetector is imported only by tests/ and archive/scripts/ — never by production cogs or team_manager — so this is currently latent dead code.
confirmed.why: If ever re-wired into team detection it would silently produce wrong rosters/substitutions; it also contradicts the documented session/round contract, so it is a correctness trap for future maintainers.
confirmed.fix_proposal: Scope both queries by gaming_session_id (join rounds on round_id) instead of `round_date LIKE`, and key rosters by a true per-match identifier (gaming_session_id + match_id/round_start_unix) rather than round_number. Until then, mark the module deprecated/unused so it is not re-enabled by mistake.

_verify.refutation: Could not refute the technical claims — all verified. Lines 171 & 209 do use `round_date LIKE $1`; DB confirms player_comprehensive_stats.round_number only ever holds {0,1,2} (5627/5922/5863), so rosters collapse to ≤3 keys per day; multiple gaming_session_id share one round_date (2026-03-25 = 4 sessions). The code, if run, would produce wrong rosters. I could not refute that the logic is broken. What tempers it: the module is genuinely dead — grep shows SubstitutionDetector is imported ONLY by tests/unit/test_substitution_detector.py, a Nov-2025 backup dir, and archive/scripts/; no production cog, team_manager.py, or service imports it. So the bug fabricates nothing today. (Finding even understates the breakage: keying by game_team/side means stopwatch side-swap R1↔R2 would flag nearly all players as sub'd — but still only if revived.)
_verify.ground_truth_check: Read full bot/core/substitution_detector.py (queries at 164-230, diff logic 232-312). Grepped whole repo for SubstitutionDetector/analyze_session_roster_changes/adjust_team_detection — only tests, backups/2025-11, archive/scripts. Verified bot/core/CLAUDE.md lists it but team_manager.py is the single team-detection source. DB: round_number distribution = {0:5627,1:5922,2:5863}; rounds grouped by round_date show 2026-03-25 has 4 sessions/75 rounds confirming date scoping mixes sessions.

confirmed.title: Midnight-crossover R1 lookup skips the previous-day search whenever any same-day R1 of the map exists, even if none precede the R2
confirmed.evidence: find_corresponding_round_1_file runs STEP 3 (previous-day glob for midnight crossings) only `if not potential_files` (line 474). potential_files is populated in STEP 2 by globbing `{date}-*-{map}-round-1.txt` for the SAME date as the R2 (lines 461-471). For an R2 whose filename date is N+1 (round ended just after midnight) whose true R1 is on date N, if any later same-map R1 already exists on date N+1 (e.g. a subsequent match during batch reprocessing/backfill), STEP 3 is skipped. The day-N+1 R1 candidates are all AFTER the R2, so the `r1_datetime < r2_datetime` filter (line 516) rejects them, best_r1_file stays None, and the function returns None — even though the correct previous-day R1 exists.
confirmed.why: When the R1 is not found, parse_round_2_with_differential stores the R2 as raw cumulative (R1+R2) instead of the differential, inflating that round's numbers. Narrow (mainly affects re-import/backfill ordering, not the live one-file-at-a-time path), hence low.
confirmed.fix_proposal: Always run the previous-day search and merge its results into potential_files (or run STEP 3 whenever STEP 2 produced no candidate strictly before the R2 within the window), rather than gating it on potential_files being empty.

_verify.refutation: I tried to refute this but the code logic holds. find_corresponding_round_1_file at bot/community_stats_parser.py:461-491 populates potential_files from STEP 2 using ONLY the R2's own date glob ({date}-*-{map}-round-1.txt), and STEP 3 (previous-day midnight glob) is gated behind `if not potential_files` (line 474). For a midnight-crossover R2 (filename date N+1, true R1 on date N) where the same map was also played later on day N+1, STEP 2 finds that later same-day R1, suppresses STEP 3, and the candidate-time filter `r1_datetime < r2_datetime` (line 516) then rejects all the after-R2 same-day candidates, so best_r1_file stays None and the function returns None. parse_round_2_with_differential (lines 542-548) then falls back to storing the R2 as raw cumulative (round_num forced to 2), inflating that round. STEP 1 (exact timestamp match, line 450) does not help across midnight since timestamps differ. The fallback path that masks the failure is real, not a defensive no-op.
_verify.ground_truth_check: Read bot/community_stats_parser.py lines 390-559 (parse dispatch, find_corresponding_round_1_file STEP 1/2/3, candidate filter, R2 differential fallback). Confirmed line citations: STEP 3 gate at 474, same-day glob 461-471, r1_datetime<r2_datetime at 516, raw-cumulative fallback at 542-548. Verified callers via grep: only bot/community_stats_parser.py:407 (live dispatch) and scripts/archive/audit_round_pairs.py:88 plus tests. Listed bot/local_stats/ (304 accumulated files across many dates/maps) confirming that a full re-parse glob can see later-dated same-map R1 files, making the bug reachable in backfill/re-import; live one-file-at-a-time path is safe because future R1 files do not yet exist on disk. Not covered by the two prior audits (those target CVEs/uploads/CI and last-month features; this is foundational parser logic).

confirmed.title: `!last_session maps` / `maps full` always crash — SQL placeholder `{map_ids_str}`/`{round_ids_str}` is never substituted
confirmed.evidence: In `show_maps_view` the query string (line 976, NOT an f-string) contains a literal CTE `SELECT id FROM rounds WHERE id IN ({map_ids_str})`, then is run via `fetch_all(query, tuple(map_session_ids))` at line 1004 with NO `.format(...)` call. `_send_round_stats` has the identical defect with `{round_ids_str}` (line 1208 / fetch_all line 1234). Every OTHER query in this file (lines 583,595,698,751,807,848,891,1396,1593) correctly calls `query.format(session_ids_str=session_ids_str)` — only these two omit it. I ran the exact CTE against Postgres via the db tool: `SELECT id FROM rounds WHERE id IN ({map_ids_str})` -> `syntax error at or near "{"`. Additionally the query has zero `?`/`$N` placeholders yet receives params, which asyncpg also rejects. Both methods are live-wired: last_session_cog.py:257-259 and session_cog.py:186-188 route `!last_session maps`, `!last_session maps full`, `!session ... maps` to them, with no surrounding try/except, so the command errors out for the user. Introduced 2026-02-28 (commit cbea046 'fix: resolve ruff lint errors') — a botched de-f-stringing that dropped both the `f` prefix and the substitution.
confirmed.fix_proposal: Mirror the working siblings: build `placeholders = ','.join('?' * len(map_session_ids))`, change the CTE to `... WHERE id IN ({placeholders})`, and call `fetch_all(query.format(placeholders=placeholders), tuple(map_session_ids))` (same for `_send_round_stats` with round_session_ids). Or drop the CTE and inline `WHERE p.round_id IN ({placeholders})`. Add a smoke test that actually executes `!last_session maps` against a seeded session so a missing `.format()` regresses CI.
confirmed.why: Two user-facing session commands have been completely broken for ~4 months; users get an error instead of map stats.

_verify.refutation: I tried to refute this on three fronts and all failed. (1) Hidden substitution at the adapter layer: bot/core/database_adapter.py only translates `?`→`$N` (_translate_placeholders, line ~438); it does no `.format()` / brace substitution, so the literal `{map_ids_str}`/`{round_ids_str}` passes straight to asyncpg and the query also has zero `?` placeholders. (2) Outer error handling that masks/recovers: last_session_cog.py has a top-level try at line 98 with except at 491, but it merely sends '❌ Error...' to the user — it does not fix the query, confirming user-facing breakage rather than refuting it. (3) Dead code / unreachable: both methods are wired — last_session_cog.py:257-259 (maps / maps full) and session_cog.py:181-186 (session ... maps / maps full). show_maps_view loops over map_matches and crashes on the first iteration whenever the session has any maps (always true with data). All sibling queries in the same file correctly call .format(session_ids_str=...), making these two the lone omissions.
_verify.ground_truth_check: Read session_view_handlers.py lines 960-1018 and 1190-1249: confirmed literal {map_ids_str} (978) and {round_ids_str} (1208) with fetch_all at 1004/1234 and no .format(). Grepped .format() usages (8 sibling queries all use it) and confirmed only 978/1208 omit it. git log -L on the query line shows commit cbea046 (2026-02-28 'fix: resolve ruff lint errors'); diffing cbea046^ vs cbea046 shows the pre-commit version had `map_ids_str = ','.join('?' * len(map_session_ids))` (line 973) + an f-string, both removed by the commit — a botched de-f-stringing. Verified adapter only handles `?`→`$N`, no brace substitution. Verified cog routing in last_session_cog.py:253-259 and session_cog.py:181-186, plus the wrapping try/except at lines 98/491 that surfaces the error to the user. Date introduced and 4-month breakage window are accurate.

confirmed.title: N+1 query in _collect_campaign_recipients (2 extra queries per recipient)
confirmed.evidence: After one fetch_all of availability_entries for a date (line 1105), the loop runs `await _load_subscription_preference_row(db, discord_user_id)` (line 1137 -> single-row SELECT on subscription_preferences) AND `await db.fetch_one(... player_links ... WHERE discord_id=$1)` (line 1160) for EVERY recipient row. So a campaign send issues 1 + 2*N round-trips. Bounded by daily availability entries (~30-50 for this community) and only triggered by a manual promoter 'send campaign' action, so real-world impact is small.
confirmed.fix_proposal: Collapse into the initial query with two LEFT JOINs: `availability_entries ae LEFT JOIN subscription_preferences sp ON sp.user_id = ae.user_id LEFT JOIN player_links pl ON pl.discord_id = ae.user_id WHERE ae.entry_date = $1 AND ae.status IN (...)`. One round-trip instead of 1+2N; per-row Python logic (channel selection, decrypt, dedup) stays unchanged.
confirmed.why: Eliminates per-recipient DB round-trips on the promotion path; cleaner and removes a latency spike that scales linearly with the night's roster.

_verify.refutation: Could not refute. The cited N+1 pattern exists exactly as described. Line 1105 does one fetch_all of availability_entries; the loop body calls _load_subscription_preference_row (defined at line 1054, a single-row SELECT on subscription_preferences) at line 1137 and db.fetch_one on player_links at line 1160 for each recipient row, yielding 1 + 2N round-trips. No batching/JOIN guard exists elsewhere. The only mitigating reality is scale: the row set is bounded by daily availability entries (~30-50 for this ~50-player community) and the path is triggered solely by a manual promoter 'send campaign' action, so practical impact is negligible. The proposed LEFT JOIN consolidation is valid and preserves per-row Python logic. The finding itself accurately self-scopes the impact as small.
_verify.ground_truth_check: Read availability.py lines 1054-1083 (_load_subscription_preference_row helper) and 1090-1186 (_collect_campaign_recipients). Confirmed: 1x fetch_all at 1105, per-row subscription_preferences fetch_one via helper at 1137, per-row player_links fetch_one at 1160. grep confirmed helper definition and call sites. Pattern matches claim precisely. Not covered by WHOLE_CODEBASE_AUDIT_2026-06-15 (scanner/CVE focus) or DEEP_AUDIT_2026-06-27 (last-month features); this is foundational backend code.

confirmed.title: Non-sargable SUBSTRING(round_date,1,10) fallback seq-scans the largest table
confirmed.evidence: In get_session_graph_stats the fallback branch (no gaming_session_id) filters `SUBSTRING(p.round_date, 1, 10) = $1` on player_comprehensive_stats. EXPLAIN ANALYZE confirms a Seq Scan on player_comprehensive_stats with 'Rows Removed by Filter: 17376' (the wrapping function defeats any index on round_date; none exists anyway). Currently 3.9ms because the table is only 17k rows, and the primary code path uses the indexed `r.gaming_session_id = $1` branch (line 688), so this only bites when the frontend omits gaming_session_id.
confirmed.fix_proposal: Prefer the gaming_session_id branch (already sargable). For the date fallback, query rounds by `round_date` (which IS indexed via idx_rounds_date) and join to PCS on the indexed round_id, e.g. filter `r.round_date LIKE $1 || '%'` / a range predicate rather than `SUBSTRING(p.round_date,...)` on the big table. Avoids the full PCS scan if the table ever grows.
confirmed.why: Removes a function-wrapped predicate on the largest table; negligible now but a cheap, correct sargable rewrite that won't degrade as history grows.

_verify.refutation: Could not refute the code claim: sessions_router.py:691 genuinely filters SUBSTRING(p.round_date,1,10)=$1 on player_comprehensive_stats in the no-gaming_session_id fallback, which is non-sargable and has no supporting index. The only thing that 'refutes' its importance is scale: 17k-row table, ~3.9ms, fallback-only path, and a community growth rate (a handful of rounds/week) that means the seq scan will never become a real problem within this project's lifetime. The sargable primary branch (r.gaming_session_id, line 688) already handles the common case.
_verify.ground_truth_check: Read sessions_router.py lines 650-758: confirmed the if/else where_clause (688 sargable gaming_session_id vs 691 SUBSTRING fallback) and the FROM player_comprehensive_stats p JOIN rounds r query. Checked tools/schema_postgresql.sql: player_comprehensive_stats.round_date is text NOT NULL with no index; rounds.round_date has idx_rounds_date (line 7599) and a composite idx_rounds_map_round_date_time (7627), confirming the proposed r.round_date LIKE prefix rewrite would be sargable. Finding's technical claims all hold.

confirmed.evidence: session_view_handlers.py and bot/services/session_graph_generator.py carry the same private helpers with effectively identical bodies: `_parse_time_to_seconds` (view 78-95 vs graph 81-99), `_row_get` (view 98-105 vs graph 101-108), `_call_service_method`+`_normalize_round_factor_payload` (view 107-162 vs graph 110-164 — diff is a single blank line), and `_get_player_stats_columns` (view 555-573 vs graph 61-80 — only a `@staticmethod` decorator differs). `diff` over view 78-162 vs graph 81-164 reports only 3 changed lines across ~85 lines. confirmed.title: Full table DDL embedded as inline Python strings duplicates tools/schema_postgresql.sql (two schema sources of truth)
confirmed.evidence: `_create_schema_if_missing` (lines 338-1002, ~660 lines) hand-writes 25 `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` statements as Python triple-quoted strings (e.g. the 57-column player_comprehensive_stats DDL at lines 393-450). The canonical tools/schema_postgresql.sql defines 101 `CREATE TABLE`s. The manager never reads that .sql file (no grep reference), so it creates only a partial subset and maintains column definitions in parallel; any column added in the .sql or a migration is invisible here and vice-versa.
confirmed.fix_proposal: Make tools/schema_postgresql.sql the single source of truth: have `_create_schema_if_missing` load and execute that file instead of inlining DDL, or delete the inline DDL for tables already defined in the .sql and document the authoritative file.

_verify.refutation: Attempted to refute by checking whether the manager loads tools/schema_postgresql.sql (it does not — only a stale, wrong-path log string references it), and whether install/bootstrap uses the .sql directly (install.sh lines 669-677 bootstraps via the manager's inline DDL, not the canonical file). Both checks confirm the duplication rather than refute it. Counts verified: 25 inline CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS in postgresql_database_manager.py vs 101 CREATE TABLE in tools/schema_postgresql.sql. Line range 338-1002 for _create_schema_if_missing is accurate (next def at 1003). The dual-source-of-truth claim stands.
_verify.ground_truth_check: Read postgresql_database_manager.py:338-450 and 880-904 (inline DDL + indexes). grep -c CREATE TABLE: 25 inline vs 101 in tools/schema_postgresql.sql. grep for schema_postgresql refs in .py: only a stale log line at 3196 pointing to wrong path bot/schema_postgresql.sql. Confirmed install.sh:669-677 initializes schema by running the manager (inline path), and 60 migration files plus _migrate_schema_if_needed (line 1003) layer additional tables. Not covered by WHOLE_CODEBASE_AUDIT_2026-06-15 or DEEP_AUDIT_2026-06-27.

confirmed.title: Second, frozen migration system competes with migrations/*.sql + scripts/apply_migrations.py
confirmed.evidence: `_migrate_schema_if_needed` (lines 1003-1344) is a hand-rolled runner with 11 numbered migrations (`# Migration 1`..`# Migration 11`, lines 1021-1316), each doing its own information_schema check + ALTER, and it runs on EVERY `connect()` (line 216) plus create_fresh (line 259). Separately the repo has the canonical migrations/ directory (60 numbered .sql files) applied by scripts/apply_migrations.py. Changes #1-#11 live in Python, #12+ in SQL files; the inline list is frozen with no shared version ledger between the two mechanisms.
confirmed.fix_proposal: Retire the inline ladder: confirm migrations 1-11 exist as numbered files under migrations/, then delete `_migrate_schema_if_needed` and route the manager through the same apply_migrations path. One runner, one ordering source.

_verify.refutation: Could not refute the core factual claims — the inline ladder, its execution sites, and the parallel migrations/*.sql + apply_migrations.py system all exist as described, and three inline migrations (3,4,8) literally duplicate SQL files 006/007/008. However, the practical harm is minimal: every inline migration is guarded by information_schema/pg_constraint existence checks (idempotent, no conflict), the inline list is frozen at 11 so divergence does not grow, and at normal bot runtime the manager reuses the bot's existing asyncpg pool (ultimate_bot.py:1019-1020) so the inline ladder is skipped — it mainly runs for the interactive CLI (main() connect() at line 3091). No data-correctness or schema-drift bug results today. The fix proposal also overstates 1:1 file correspondence (numbering is offset, and inline #1/#2/#10/#11 lack clean SQL twins).
_verify.ground_truth_check: Read postgresql_database_manager.py:1003-1344 (full inline ladder, 11 migrations all with existence guards), :200-274 (connect() line 216 and create_fresh_database line 259 call sites), and confirmed CLI main() connect() at line 3091. Listed migrations/ (60 .sql files) and confirmed content overlap by reading headers of 006_add_full_selfkills.sql, 007_add_round_id_to_lua_round_teams.sql, 008_add_bot_round_flags.sql against inline Migrations 3/4/8. confirmed.title: Overlapping session-summary endpoints each re-derive map_counts/scoring instead of sharing one path
confirmed.evidence: sessions_router.py (1931 lines) exposes ~6 overlapping session-summary endpoints: /stats/last-session (129), /stats/session-leaderboard (346), /stats/session-score/{date} (395), /sessions/{date} (567), /stats/sessions (1180), /stats/session/{gaming_session_id}/detail (1379). Some key by date (fetch_session_data_by_date), some by gaming_session_id, despite the project rule 'ALWAYS use gaming_session_id (NOT dates)'. The map_counts accumulation loop is copy-pasted at 162-164 and 644-646. build_session_scoring is already factored out (good) but the fetch+aggregate+score sequence repeats per endpoint.

confirmed.title: 50-entry MAP_IMAGE_MAP + map helpers duplicated verbatim across 3 production JS files (asset drift)
confirmed.evidence: `const MAP_IMAGE_MAP`, `function normalizeMapKey`, `function mapImageFor`, `function mapLabel` are duplicated byte-for-byte in js/sessions.js, js/session-detail.js and js/sessions2.js (grep: MAP_IMAGE_MAP defined in 3 files; normalizeMapKey in 4 incl. proximity.js; mapImageFor in 3; formatDuration in 5 files: live-status, sessions, session-detail, season-stats, proximity). Adding/renaming a map (a recurring task — the map list grows) requires editing the same 50-line table in 3+ places; missing one yields a generic-tile fallback in one view but the real thumbnail in another, an inconsistency users see directly.
confirmed.fix_proposal: Extract the map-image table + normalizeMapKey/mapImageFor/mapLabel/formatDuration into a shared module (e.g. js/map-utils.js, alongside existing js/utils.js) and import from sessions.js, session-detail.js, sessions2.js, proximity.js. No framework change; pure de-dup of an already-modular ESM codebase.

_verify.refutation: Could not refute the existence of duplication (MAP_IMAGE_MAP + normalizeMapKey/mapImageFor/mapLabel present in 3 JS files; formatDuration in 5), but DID refute the finding's central characterization. The claim 'duplicated byte-for-byte' is false: I diffed the blocks and sessions.js uses assets/maps/proximity/*.png while session-detail.js/sessions2.js use assets/maps/levelshots/*.png and carry 6 extra base-map keys (battery/fueldump/goldrush/oasis/radar/railgun). Both directories exist on disk and hold different images, so the two tables are intentionally divergent, not identical. formatDuration likewise already diverged (only sessions.js+session-detail.js hash-match; live-status/season-stats/proximity each differ). Consequence: the proposed 'extract one shared module' fix is not drop-in — it must parameterize the image directory or it changes which thumbnails render. The DRY concern is genuine and drift is already manifested (sessions.js lacks plain goldrush/oasis/fueldump/railgun keys -> generic-tile fallback that session-detail resolves), but the severity and the 'byte-for-byte' premise are overstated.

confirmed.title: Running map score overlay desyncs when a map has an odd round count (orphan R1/R2)
confirmed.evidence: numPlays = Math.max(1, Math.floor(mapMatch.rounds.length / 2)) assumes every map play is exactly R1+R2. For each play it advances scoringMapIdx through scoringMaps and accumulates runningA/runningB (1685-1690). When a map block contains an odd number of rounds (orphan R2 / abandoned R1 — a documented condition in this codebase, e.g. memory notes 'orphan R2' and stopwatch_pairing), floor() under- or mis-counts the plays consumed, so scoringMapIdx drifts and the cumulative A:B badge shown on this and every subsequent map is attributed to the wrong map.
confirmed.why: Produces a visibly wrong running scoreboard for any session containing an orphan/abandoned round, which is exactly the edge case this community hits.
confirmed.fix_proposal: Match scoring entries to rounds by a stable key (map_name + round_start_unix or round_id) instead of floor(rounds.length/2); or count only completed R1+R2 pairs and skip orphan rounds when advancing scoringMapIdx.

_verify.refutation: Could not fully refute: the drift mechanism is provable. /stats/session/{id}/detail (sessions_router.py:1463-1478) groups a standalone orphan round into a 1-round match block; scoring.maps excludes orphans (stopwatch_scoring_service.py:259-263); and sessions.js:1680 forces numPlays = Math.max(1, floor(1/2)) = 1, so it consumes a scoring entry that doesn't belong to that block, over-advancing scoringMapIdx and mis-attributing the running A:B badge (rendered at line 1744) for that and subsequent maps. BUT the finding is overstated/partly misattributed: (1) the common odd case — a 3-round block (R1+R2+stray-R2 or R1+R1+R2) — yields floor(3/2)=1, which correctly matches the single complete pair scored, so it does NOT drift; only standalone 1-round orphan blocks (or pathological R1R1R2R2 orderings) drift. (2) The cited root cause is slightly wrong: floor() correctly returns 0 for a 1-round block; it is the Math.max(1, ...) minimum-of-1 that over-counts. Net symptom still matches.
_verify.ground_truth_check: Read sessions.js:1600-1755 (numPlays heuristic + scoringMapIdx accumulation + badge render at 1744, and authoritative series header at 1628-1629 using scoring.team_a_score directly). Read sessions_router.py get_stats_session_detail (1379-1484): matches grouped by consecutive same-map rounds, new match when R1 follows R2 — so normal blocks are exactly R1+R2. Read bot/services/stopwatch_scoring_service.py (204-263): scoring.maps filtered to complete R1+R2 pairs only, orphans excluded. Confirmed standalone-orphan map blocks (documented in this codebase: ~56 abandoned_r1 + ~49 orphan_r2 historically) cause the running badge to drift. Authoritative header score is unaffected. Not covered by the two prior audits.

confirmed.title: BOX scoring collapses orphan/duplicate R2 rounds into one map_number, silently dropping real rounds and pairing mismatched maps
confirmed.evidence: _fetch_session_rounds() assigns map_number by incrementing ONLY when round_number==1 ('if round_num == 1: map_number += 1'), then calculate_session_score() groups with 'maps.setdefault(r.map_number, {})[r.round_number] = r'. When a session contains R2 rounds with no preceding R1 (orphan R2) or two R2s in a row, every such R2 keeps the previous map_number and overwrites dict slot [2]. DB confirms this is common: gaming_session_id 51 is 5 consecutive orphan R2 rows (supply, te_escape2, sw_goldrush_te, etl_frostbite, erdenberg_t2, all round_number=2, no R1), so all 5 land on map_number=0; maps[0] keeps only the LAST R2 and score_map returns immediately because r1 is None -> the entire session (5 decisive maps incl. a Fullhold and Completeds) scores 0-0. A query for R2 whose prior round in time-order is also R2/none found 9 affected sessions (ids 99,51,84 each with 5; 50,41,44,56,47,42 with 2). In mixed sessions the surviving R2 is paired with an R1 from a DIFFERENT map, so winner_team is checked against the wrong alpha_side and the map score is fabricated.
confirmed.fix_proposal: Pair rounds deterministically by the existing match/round-linker key (map_name + match_id / round_start_unix), not 'increment on R1'. At minimum start a new map_number whenever round_number==1 OR the current map_number already holds a round_number==2 (so a second R2 starts a fresh bucket), and skip/flag orphan R2 instead of overwriting. Surface a 'rounds_dropped' count in the API response.
confirmed.why: BOX score is the canonical 'who won the evening' number shown on /tonight and session pages; dropping real R2 rounds or pairing mismatched maps produces a wrong scoreline, violating the no-fabricated-numbers rule.

_verify.refutation:
I tried to refute this finding and could not. The code at box_scoring_service.py:240-266 (_fetch_session_rounds) increments map_number ONLY when round_num==1 ('if round_num == 1: map_number += 1'), and calculate_session_score (line 135) groups via 'maps.setdefault(r.map_number, {})[r.round_number] = r', which overwrites slot [2] for every consecutive R2. Both failure modes reproduce against live data:

(1) Orphan-only collapse -> 0-0: gaming_session_id 51 (2025-05-10) has exactly 5 rounds with round_number=2 and NO R1 rows at all (only round_number 0 and 2 exist; ids 9294/9296/9298/9300/9302 = supply, te_escape2, sw_goldrush_te, etl_frostbite, erdenberg_t2, including a Fullhold and two Completeds). All keep map_number=0, maps[0] retains only the last R2, and score_map hits 'if not r1: continue' (line 141) -> session scores 0-0, maps_completed=0, winner=None. Decisive maps silently dropped.

(2) Mismatched-map pairing -> fabricated score: gaming_session_id 41 traces to map_number=1 holding R1=etl_adlernest (id 9200) paired with R2=etl_sp_delivery (id 9205, an orphan R2), because supply R2 (9203) then delivery R2 (9205) overwrite the real adlernest R2 (9201) in slot [2]. The MapScore is labeled etl_adlernest but scored from etl_sp_delivery's winner_team, and adlernest's actual R2 (Fullhold) plus supply's R2 are dropped. winner_team is thus checked against the wrong alpha_side for a different map -> fabricated scoreline.

The endpoint is live: GET /api/storytelling/box-score?session_date=... (storytelling_router.py:464-484) calls calculate_session_score directly, so any historical session page hitting these dates returns the wrong score. The only correction to the finding's numbers: it claimed 9 affected sessions; the real count is 21 sessions with >=1 orphan/double R2 (ids 51,84,99 with 5 each; 41,42,44,47,50,56 with 2; plus 11 with 1). Direction and severity of the bug are understated, not overstated.
_verify.ground_truth_check: Read box_scoring_service.py lines 1-269 (full scoring + _fetch_session_rounds). Confirmed map_number increments only on round_number==1 and the dict overwrite on [round_number]. Confirmed score_map's 'if not r1: continue' path. Verified the endpoint wiring: grep shows calculate_session_score is called from storytelling_router.py:483 (live /storytelling/box-score endpoint). DB queries: session 51 has only round_number 0+2 rows (no R1) for 5 distinct maps, all is_valid=true; session 41 interleaves real R1/R2 pairs with two orphan R2 rows (9203 supply, 9205 delivery) between the adlernest R1/R2 pair, proving the slot-[2] overwrite collapses three maps into one bucket. A LAG window query counted 21 sessions (not 9) with an R2 whose prior in-session round is R2/none. confirmed.title: Per-session skill rating keyed on round_date conflates multiple distinct gaming sessions played on the same calendar day
confirmed.evidence: compute_session_ratings(), compute_session_map_ratings() and get_player_session_history() scope a 'session' with 'WHERE ... round_date = $2' / 'round_date <= $2' (TEXT date), and the docstring calls round_date a 'gaming session proxy'. This violates the project CRITICAL RULE 'ALWAYS use gaming_session_id for session queries (NOT dates)'. DB shows the conflation is real: round_date 2026-03-25 maps to 4 distinct gaming_session_id values, and 2026-06-21/06-16/06-11/05-27/2025-12-21/2025-05-11/2025-01-07 each map to 2. On those days the 'session rating' merges 2-4 separate evenings' aggregates (SUM(kills)/COUNT rounds across all of them), producing a per-session number and history delta that correspond to no actual gaming session.
confirmed.fix_proposal: Scope these queries by gaming_session_id (join rounds r ON pcs.round_id = r.id and filter r.gaming_session_id), and key history/percentile lookups on gaming_session_id rather than round_date. If a date-based API contract must be kept, return one entry per gaming_session_id within the date.
confirmed.why: Directly contradicts the documented session rule and yields merged, non-reproducible per-session ratings on 8+ known dates (worst case 4 sessions in one day).

_verify.refutation: I tried to refute this but could not. The code at skill_rating_service.py:440-481 (compute_session_ratings), 498-537 (compute_session_map_ratings) and 554-575 (get_player_session_history) all scope a "session" via WHERE player_guid=$1 AND round_date=$2 (and round_date>=... for history), never via gaming_session_id. The docstring at line 443 explicitly admits round_date is a "gaming session proxy", and skill_router.py:204-214 exposes this as a date-keyed session drill-down (session_date ISO date). This directly violates the documented CRITICAL RULE "ALWAYS use gaming_session_id for session queries (NOT dates)". I confirmed pcs/rounds carry gaming_session_id, so the correct grouping is available. The only mitigating angle (that a per-calendar-day sparkline is a defensible UX choice) does not hold because the API and function names assert "session" semantics, not "day".
_verify.ground_truth_check: Read skill_rating_service.py:400-599 and skill_router.py:180-225 to confirm the date-based scoping and the public contract. Ran read-only DB queries against `rounds`: 8 of 148 distinct round_dates map to >1 gaming_session_id (2026-03-25 → 4 sessions; 2025-05-11, 2025-12-21, 2026-05-27, 2026-06-11, 2026-06-16, 2025-01-07, 2026-06-21 → 2 each). Total 128 gaming sessions across 148 dates. So on those 8 dates (~5.4%) the per-session rating and history delta SUM/AVG-merge 2-4 separate evenings, matching the finding exactly.

confirmed.title: pcs_only rating scaling leaves CONSTANT unscaled, so session ratings are systematically below global ratings (contradicting the stated intent)
confirmed.evidence: calculate_et_rating() scales metric weights by 'scale = 1.0 / pcs_weight_sum' for pcs_only but leaves CONSTANT=0.15 unscaled; the comment claims this makes 'an average PCS-only player score ~0.50, same as an average global player.' Working it through: global average player ~ 0.15 + 0.5*sum(all signed weights=0.84) = 0.57; pcs_only average ~ 0.15 + 0.5*(scale*pcs_signed_sum=0.75) = 0.525. So session/map ratings sit ~0.045 below the global average and below the documented 0.50 center, the exact systematic offset the scaling was meant to remove, which also shifts get_tier() boundaries for session/map views.
confirmed.fix_proposal: Scale CONSTANT consistently in pcs_only mode, or recompute CONSTANT/scale so both global and pcs_only average players land on the same target (e.g. 0.50). Add a unit test asserting an all-0.5-percentile player yields the same rating in both modes.
confirmed.why: Cross-scope comparisons (global vs session/map tiers) are biased, undermining trust in the rating, though magnitude is small.

_verify.refutation: I tried to refute by re-deriving the weight sums from WEIGHTS (lines 32-54) and checking whether re-baselining washes out the offset. It does not: session/map scope (calculate_et_rating pcs_only=True at lines 487/542/621) uses the SAME global population percentiles (compute_population_percentiles, line 505), so a player whose session stats equal their global average lands at ~0.5 percentile in both modes yet rates 0.525 (pcs_only) vs 0.57 (global) — a real ~0.045 systematic offset. The math in the finding is exactly correct. get_tier() is shared across scopes, so the shift can flip tiers near the 0.55 boundary. The docstring (lines 203-206) explicitly claims both average players land ~0.50, which is false on two counts (global is 0.57, pcs_only is 0.525). Could not refute.
_verify.ground_truth_check: Read skill_rating_service.py lines 32-99 (WEIGHTS, CONSTANT=0.15, PCS/PROXIMITY metric sets, TIERS, get_tier) and 198-242 (calculate_et_rating scaling). Verified pcs_weight_sum=0.64 (abs), PCS signed sum=0.48, total signed=0.84. Confirmed callers: session rating line 487, per-map 542, cumulative 621 all use pcs_only=True with global percentiles (line 505). get_tier shared in skill_router.py and players_profile_router.py. Not duplicated in the two prior audits (scanner pass / last-month feature pass).

confirmed.title: Skill history endpoint is N+1 with O(N^2) cumulative full-history rescans (range_days up to 3650)
confirmed.evidence: get_player_session_history() iterates every distinct round_date for a player and, per date, fires TWO awaited queries: compute_session_ratings() (one per-date aggregate, line 584) and a cumulative aggregate that rescans ALL rows WHERE round_date <= $2 (lines 589-615). The cumulative slice grows every iteration, so total work is O(rounds^2). It is exposed publicly/uncached via GET /skill/player/{id}/history, and skill_router.py:197 clamps range_days only at 3650 (UI offers an all-time option). DB ground truth: top player D8423F90 has 126 distinct round_dates / 1506 rows -> a single all-time request fans out to ~253 sequential queries and re-aggregates ~95k row-slices. There is no composite index either: only idx_player_stats_guid (player_guid alone) exists, so each cumulative query filters round_date in-heap. The 30-day default keeps the common case small (~8-12 dates), but the worst case is a public request.
confirmed.fix_proposal: Replace the per-date loop with a single GROUP BY round_date query to get all per-session aggregates in one pass, and compute the cumulative line with SQL window functions (running SUMs over round_date) or a single cumulative scan in Python, instead of one cumulative query per date. Alternatively cache the result per (guid, range_days) with a short TTL like the moments cache. Optionally add a (player_guid, round_date) index if cumulative SQL is retained.
confirmed.why: A single unauthenticated all-time request issues hundreds of sequential awaited queries and quadratic row work; on the shared FastAPI worker this blocks other requests far longer than the small data set warrants.

_verify.refutation: Could not refute the structural claims — all verified against code and DB. The loop at skill_rating_service.py:580-628 genuinely fires two awaited queries per distinct round_date, and the cumulative query (lines 589-615) rescans a round_date<=$2 slice that grows each iteration, producing O(rounds^2) work. range_days is clamped only at 3650 (skill_router.py:197), the /skill/player/{id}/history endpoint has no auth and no cache (only Depends(get_db); grep for cache/lru/ttl returns nothing), and only idx_player_stats_guid (player_guid alone) exists — no (player_guid, round_date) composite. The one overstatement is impact: each cumulative query is bounded by the player's own ~1520 rows via the player_guid index (not a 95k full scan), and the per-iteration awaits let asyncio interleave other requests rather than hard-blocking the worker, so worst-case wall time is roughly sub-second on localhost.
_verify.ground_truth_check: Read skill_rating_service.py lines 440-630 (compute_session_ratings, get_player_session_history) and skill_router.py lines 170-225 (the public history endpoint, range_days clamp, no cache). Grepped schema_postgresql.sql: confirmed only idx_player_stats_guid (player_guid) and idx_player_stats_round (round_id) on player_comprehensive_stats — no (player_guid, round_date) composite. DB query confirmed top player D8423F90 has 127 distinct round_dates / 1520 rows (finding's 126/1506 essentially correct), so an all-time request fans out to ~254 sequential queries. Verified percentiles are computed once (line 562), not per-iteration, so that part is not an additional N+1.

confirmed.title: compute_all_ratings scans proximity_kill_outcome twice for two derivable metrics
confirmed.evidence: Two separate LEFT JOIN subqueries both full-scan proximity_kill_outcome grouped by killer_guid_canonical: prox_quality (AVG gib-weighted outcome, lines 312-324) and prox_perm (gib_rate = COUNT FILTER gibbed / COUNT, lines 333-340). Both kill_quality and gib_rate are computable in a single grouped pass over the same table. This is the all-player rating rebuild (compute_and_store_ratings, admin/scheduled), so it runs infrequently, but it doubles the scan of one of the larger proximity tables on every rebuild.
confirmed.fix_proposal: Merge prox_quality and prox_perm into one subquery selecting both AVG(CASE outcome...) and COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE outcome='gibbed')::REAL / NULLIF(COUNT(*),0) grouped by killer_guid_canonical, and join once.
confirmed.why: Halves one redundant full-table aggregation in the rating rebuild; cheap correctness-neutral win.

_verify.refutation: Could not refute the factual claim: lines 312-324 (prox_quality) and 333-340 (prox_perm) are indeed two distinct LEFT JOIN subqueries each performing a full grouped scan of proximity_kill_outcome on killer_guid_canonical, and both derived metrics (kill_quality AVG-of-CASE, gib_rate COUNT FILTER/COUNT) are computable in a single pass. The only mitigant is impact, not correctness: it is an infrequent admin/scheduled all-player rebuild over a small table (~29k rows), so the redundant scan costs milliseconds. No guard or reason makes the duplication necessary — they could simply be one subquery.
_verify.ground_truth_check: Read skill_rating_service.py lines 280-369: confirmed both subqueries scan proximity_kill_outcome grouped by killer_guid_canonical with the exact predicates described, joined separately as prox_quality and prox_perm, and both columns referenced in the SELECT/GROUP BY. Queried the DB: proximity_kill_outcome has 29,094 rows (all with non-null killer_guid_canonical), confirming it is a small table and the double scan is cheap. No two prior audits cover this specific micro-optimization.

confirmed.title: 4 production React routes depend on a gitignored, untracked, 3-month-stale build; absence renders a user-facing "Modern Route Offline" error panel
confirmed.evidence: route-registry.js marks exactly 4 viewIds as VIEW_MODE.MODERN (lines 168,181,194,291: proximity-player, proximity-replay, proximity-teams, skill-rating). These mount React from /static/modern/route-host.js. But `git ls-files website/static/modern/` returns 0 (the dir is gitignored: .gitignore:49 `website/static/modern/`). The on-disk build is dated Mar 31 09:01 while SkillRating.tsx has commits on Apr 1 (8d3ef81, 8bd889b 'ET Rating v2') AFTER the build — so the deployed skill-rating page is stale by the entire ET Rating v2 change. If prod is deployed without a manual `vite build` (and the artifact is not in git), loadModernRuntime() fails and renderUnavailable() (modern-route-host.js:35) shows users a literal 'Modern Route Offline / The modern renderer is not built yet' panel (modern-route-host.js:38) instead of the page.
confirmed.fix_proposal: Pick ONE canonical surface for these 4 routes. Lowest-risk given 'legacy JS is production' + no-page-proliferation: fold them back to legacy (player-profile.js already renders the proximity-player aim/hit-region/heatmap surfaces; proximity.js covers replay/teams) and flip their mode to VIEW_MODE.LEGACY, then delete the modern-route-host plumbing. If React must stay, add `website/static/modern/` to a build step in deploy (deploy_release.sh) and to CI so the artifact is never missing/stale, and check the build into the release path. Do not leave a gitignored build as the only renderer for live routes.
confirmed.why: Users on skill-rating or proximity sub-pages can get a stale page or a raw 'Offline' error panel depending on whether someone remembered to run vite build on the box; this is exactly the silent-deploy-gap class that already caused the 2026-06-25 'Offline' incident.

_verify.refutation: I tried to refute this by hunting for (a) the build being tracked in git, (b) a deploy/CI step that builds it, or (c) the routes falling back to legacy — none exist. All core claims hold: route-registry.js sets exactly 4 routes to VIEW_MODE.MODERN (proximity-player:168, proximity-replay:181, proximity-teams:194, skill-rating:291), which load from /static/modern/ (vite.config.ts:6 outputs to ../static/modern). `git ls-files website/static/modern/` returns 0 files; .gitignore:49 lists `website/static/modern/`. modern-route-host.js:35-48 renderUnavailable() renders the literal "Modern Route Offline / The modern renderer is not built yet" panel on import failure (lines 108-110 catch). The on-disk build is dated 2026-03-31 09:01, while the ET Rating v2 source (commit f63c3bb + Codacy fixes 8bd889b/8d3ef81) landed 2026-04-01 — AFTER the build, so the dev artifact is provably stale w.r.t. SkillRating.tsx. I could NOT fully confirm prod is currently broken (the dev box still has a valid route-host.js bundle present), so the user-facing failure is conditional on a deploy that skips the manual `vite build` — which is plausible given the precedent but not observed live.
_verify.ground_truth_check: Confirmed: grep of scripts/deploy_release.sh shows ZERO frontend/vite/npm/build/modern references; no .github/workflows file references vite/npm run build/static/modern; package.json only has a manual `build: vite build`. So nothing automates building the gitignored artifact — it relies entirely on someone running vite build on the box. Neither prior audit (docs/research/WHOLE_CODEBASE_AUDIT_2026-06-15.md, docs/research/DEEP_AUDIT_2026-06-27/) mentions static/modern, modern-route-host, or VIEW_MODE.MODERN, so this is novel and not double-reported. The 2026-06-25 "Offline" incident in memory was a different mechanism (missing tracked legacy JS modules via partial deploy), but it confirms the silent-deploy-gap failure class is real for this project.

confirmed.title: React route-mode catalog contradicts the production router (claims ~19 'modern' routes; reality is 4) — abandoned strangler migration left misleading state
confirmed.evidence: catalog.ts declares mode:'modern' for home, leaderboards, maps, records, awards, profile, weapons, proximity, hall-of-fame, retro-viz, sessions2, session-detail, greatshot, greatshot-demo, uploads, upload-detail, availability, admin, skill-rating (~19). _verify.refutation: The literal claim is accurate: website/frontend/src/runtime/catalog.ts:18-42 lists 19 routes as mode:'modern', while the authoritative router website/js/route-registry.js marks only 4 as VIEW_MODE.MODERN (proximity-player, proximity-replay, proximity-teams, skill-rating) and everything else (including 'proximity' itself, 'home', 'leaderboards', etc.) as VIEW_MODE.LEGACY dispatching loadXView(). confirmed.title: ~21 React page components (~10k LOC) duplicate live legacy-JS surfaces but are unreachable in production — divergence means fixes land in only one twin
confirmed.evidence: route-host.tsx maps 25 viewIds to lazy React pages, but route-registry.js routes all but 4 to legacy JS, so ~21 React pages are compiled into static/modern/chunks (Mar 31) yet never mounted. Delete the unreachable React page set (or move frontend/ to an archived/ dir clearly marked non-production) and keep only what backs the 4 still-modern routes until those are folded to legacy. _verify.ground_truth_check: Read website/frontend/src/route-host.tsx: routeComponents maps 25 viewIds to lazy React pages (lines 13-39). Read website/js/route-registry.js: only 4 routes carry mode:VIEW_MODE.MODERN (proximity-player L168, proximity-replay L181, proximity-teams L194, skill-rating L291); all others are VIEW_MODE.LEGACY with load:({legacy})=>legacy.* handlers. confirmed.title: Inconsistent / stale cache-bust versioning across the dual frontend invites Cloudflare 24h drift
confirmed.evidence: modern-route-host.js:1 pins BUILD_VERSION='20260323-skill-rating-v1' (Mar 23) for the React entry/CSS — older than both the on-disk build (Mar 31) and source (Apr 1), so a content change would not bust the `?v=` query. Meanwhile index.html and app.js use ad-hoc per-file strings: app.js?v=20260623-aimlock, route-registry.js?v=20260513-v142-cf-bust, player-profile.js?v=20260608-aimv2, smart-stats-diag.js?v=20260607-aim, proximity.js?v=20260623-aimlock. There is no single release version; each file is bumped by hand. The memory note feedback_cloudflare_cache_buster and the 2026-06-25 incident both record CF caching JS for 24h on a fixed ?v=.
confirmed.fix_proposal: Adopt one release-version constant (e.g. read package version / git short-sha) appended as `?v=` to ALL JS/CSS including the modern build entry, set once per release in deploy_release.sh. Drop the hand-maintained per-file strings so a release can never ship a page that loads a stale module.
confirmed.why: Mixed/stale cache-bust strings are the documented root cause of UI drift and the 'Offline' incident on slomix.fyi; unifying them prevents users seeing half-old, half-new JS after a deploy.

_verify.refutation: I tried to refute this and the finding's HEADLINE framing is partly wrong, but a real (and sharper) bug survives. WRONG part of the claim: the reviewer says the per-file `?v=` strings are "bumped by hand" with "no single release version." That is refuted by scripts/deploy_release.sh step 3b (lines 299-308), which at every deploy runs `sed -i "s|?v=[A-Za-z0-9._-]\+|?v=$SHA|g" website/index.html website/js/*.js` plus injection seds — i.e. it ALREADY rewrites every literal `?v=...` in index.html and all js/*.js to the current git SHA uniformly. So the committed strings (20260623-aimlock, 20260513-v142-cf-bust, etc.) are just last-committed state; at deploy they all collapse to one $SHA. The reviewer's proposed fix ("adopt one release-version constant set once per release in deploy_release.sh") therefore largely already exists.\n\nWhat SURVIVES and is genuinely real: the modern/React build entry is the one file the auto-buster cannot reach. website/js/modern-route-host.js:1-3 builds its URLs as `/static/modern/route-host.js?v=${BUILD_VERSION}` with `BUILD_VERSION='20260323-skill-rating-v1'`. I copied the file to /tmp and ran the exact deploy sed against it: the line is unchanged, because the sed pattern `?v=[A-Za-z0-9._-]+` does not match `?v=${...}` (next char is `$`), and the BUILD_VERSION literal has no `?v=` prefix. So the modern entry ships a FIXED cache key on EVERY deploy. The on-disk build (website/static/modern/route-host.js + chunks/) is dated 20260331, already newer than the Mar-23 version string, confirming a content change that the cache key never reflected. The internal chunks are Vite content-hashed (route-host-BN5ST2qc.js, etc.), so route-host.js's 88-byte import line changes when the build changes — but if CF serves the stale-keyed route-host.js, it imports an old (possibly deleted) chunk hash → 404 → broken route. That is exactly the 2026-06-25 incident class (a single 404 ES module breaking the app). 4 routes use VIEW_MODE.MODERN (route-registry.js:168,181,194,291), so this path is live, though it is the secondary frontend (legacy JS is primary).
_verify.ground_truth_check: Read website/js/modern-route-host.js:1-3 (BUILD_VERSION + template-literal URLs). Read website/index.html (4 ?v= refs) and grepped app.js/session-detail.js for per-file version strings. Inspected scripts/deploy_release.sh lines 242-308 (step 3b auto-buster seds). Reproduced the deploy sed in /tmp against a copy of modern-route-host.js — confirmed the template-literal `?v=${BUILD_VERSION}` is NOT rewritten. Listed website/static/modern/ (route-host.js/css dated 20260331, chunks content-hashed) confirming build is newer than the Mar-23 BUILD_VERSION. Counted VIEW_MODE.MODERN routes in route-registry.js (4 live modern routes at 168/181/194/291).

confirmed.title: Display/business logic duplicated across both stacks; the React copy diverges silently because it is not served
confirmed.evidence: Same domain logic implemented twice: color-strip / name cleaning in legacy js/matches.js and js/session-detail.js AND in React frontend/src/pages/Proximity.tsx and components/story/PlayerStoryCard.tsx (grep stripColor/cleanName/shortGuid). For LEGACY-routed pages, the React equivalent (SessionDetail.tsx, Proximity.tsx) never executes in production, so a scoring/formatting fix in the legacy file has no signal forcing the React twin to update — divergence is invisible. dropped.title: R2 stored as raw cumulative (no differential, no marker) when the R1 file cannot be found
dropped.evidence: When find_corresponding_round_1_file returns None, the parser logs a warning and does `result = self.parse_regular_stats_file(round_2_file_path); result['round_num'] = 2; return result` (lines 545-548). This result is the CUMULATIVE R1+R2 file content and, unlike the real differential path (line 975 sets 'differential_calculation': True), carries no flag distinguishing it from a genuine R2. It is inserted into rounds/player_comprehensive_stats identically to a normal R2 (round_status ends up 'completed'; DB shows no 'orphan_r2'/'abandoned' status — only completed/cancelled/substitution exist). The only protection observed is per-query: leaderboard_cog.py guards with `NOT (round_number=2 AND NOT EXISTS (R1 same map+session))`. Consumers that do not replicate that guard (website endpoints, skill rating, records, session aggregators) read the inflated cumulative R2 totals. This is the parser-level root cause of the orphan-R2 inflation noted in docs/AUDIT_2026-05-29; the new angle is that there is no DB-level marker so each consumer must re-implement the guard.
dropped.why: Directly undermines the 'no fabricated numbers' invariant for any consumer lacking the ad-hoc orphan-R2 filter; a cumulative R2 roughly doubles a player's per-round kills/damage/time for that round.
dropped.fix_proposal: Persist an explicit marker on the round when the differential could not be computed (e.g. round_status='orphan_r2' or a boolean is_cumulative_r2 column) and have all stat consumers exclude or correct such rows centrally, instead of relying on each query's NOT EXISTS subquery.

_verify.refutation: The code behavior is genuine and I confirmed every claim: parser lines 542-548 store cumulative R2 as a round_num=2 result with no differential flag when R1 is missing; the DB has 36 such orphan R2 rounds all at round_status='completed' with no marker on the rounds table; and only leaderboard_cog carries the NOT EXISTS orphan guard while skill_rating/records/sessions consumers (round_number>0 or round_status-only filters) read the inflated cumulative totals. So I cannot refute the technical substance. What I CAN refute is the novelty: this is a near-verbatim re-report of FM4 in docs/AUDIT_2026-05-29.md (already marked VERIFIED/High), which cites the identical parser:541-548 lines, quantifies the same ~+23-26% inflation, and already proposes both the is_orphan_r2 marker AND the refined fix of reusing round_correlations linkage. The finding's advertised 'new angle' (no DB marker, per-consumer guard duplication) is itself already discussed in that audit. Minor inaccuracy: a partial DB-level orphan marker DOES exist (round_assemblies.orphan_r2, round_correlations.r1_round_id IS NULL) — just not on the rounds table.
_verify.ground_truth_check: Read bot/community_stats_parser.py:500-609 (orphan path 542-548, real diff flag at 975). Grepped round_status/orphan markers across bot/, website/backend/, schema, migrations. Confirmed rounds.round_status has no orphan value; round_assemblies.orphan_r2 and round_correlations.r1_round_id exist as separate correlation-layer markers. DB queries: round_status distribution (2498 completed/151 cancelled/15 substitution) and 36 orphan R2 rounds (R2 with no R1 same map+session). Verified only leaderboard_cog applies the NOT EXISTS guard; skill_rating_service uses round_number>0 only; records_seasons/sessions_router use round_status filter only. Read docs/AUDIT_2026-05-29.md lines 224-394 — FM4 already documents this exact issue (same lines, same inflation, same proposed fix and refinement).

dropped.title: round_correlations.map_name has no index; Strategy 1/2 lookups seq-scan + sort on every pipeline event
dropped.evidence: _find_nearby_correlation_id runs `SELECT correlation_id, match_id, r1_round_id FROM round_correlations WHERE map_name = ? ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20` (and a second variant for Strategy 2). schema_postgresql.sql defines only idx_round_corr_match_id, idx_round_corr_status, and the r1_r2 partial unique index — there is NO index on map_name nor on created_at. This query fires once per source event (on_round_imported / on_lua_teams_stored / on_gametime_processed / on_endstats_processed / on_proximity_imported), i.e. 4-8+ times per match, each doing a full seq scan of round_correlations plus a sort. Verified table size via DB: round_correlations = 799 rows.
dropped.fix_proposal: Add `CREATE INDEX idx_round_corr_map_created ON round_correlations (map_name, created_at DESC);` to back both the WHERE filter and the ORDER BY/LIMIT. _verify.refutation: The factual claims are all correct (code matches, no map_name/created_at index exists), but there is no real performance defect. At 799 rows the Postgres planner seq-scans regardless of any index — a btree on such a tiny table would not even be used — so the proposed fix is a no-op in practice. The reviewer themselves admit it is sub-millisecond and 'not urgent, do not treat as a live bottleneck.' Growth is ~50 rows/month, so even years out the table stays trivially small. Per project anti-over-engineering guidance (judge against real ~50-user scale, do not forward-proof), this is a cleanliness suggestion, not an issue worth acting on.

dropped.title: gaming_session_id gap computed against the GLOBAL latest round, not the chronological neighbor — out-of-order/backfill imports mislabel sessions
dropped.evidence: `_get_or_create_gaming_session_id` selects the single most-recent stored round (`ORDER BY round_date DESC, round_time DESC LIMIT 1`) and computes `gap_minutes = (current_datetime - last_datetime)/60`, then `if gap_minutes > 60: new session else: continue last_gaming_session_id`. This is only correct when files arrive in strict chronological order. If a round OLDER than the latest stored round is imported (delayed file, re-import, or a backfill run), the gap is negative, so `gap_minutes > 60` is False and the old round is silently assigned the *newest* gaming_session_id (line 2110). Because the INSERT uses `ON CONFLICT (match_id, round_number) DO UPDATE SET gaming_session_id = EXCLUDED.gaming_session_id` (line 2185), a re-import can also overwrite a previously-correct session id with the wrong one. Session-scoped stats group by gaming_session_id, so a mislabeled round inflates/contaminates whichever session it lands in.
dropped.fix_proposal: Compute the session boundary relative to the chronological neighbor of the round being inserted, not the global max: query the nearest round with `round_datetime < current` (and optionally the nearest after) and assign/split based on that gap; if a round lands between two existing rounds of different sessions, derive the id from its neighbors rather than always appending to the latest. At minimum, guard against negative gaps (treat |gap|>threshold or any backward jump as 'needs explicit session resolution') and log a warning instead of silently continuing the latest session.
dropped.why: Live in-order imports are fine, but any backfill/re-import or delayed file can silently bucket rounds into the wrong gaming session, corrupting per-session aggregates that the whole site keys on.

_verify.refutation: The code at postgresql_database_manager.py:2071-2110 does compute the 60-min gap against the GLOBAL latest stored round (ORDER BY round_date DESC, round_time DESC LIMIT 1) rather than the chronological neighbor, so the algorithm is fragile in the abstract. But every actual code path feeds files chronologically and re-imports are guarded, refuting the finding's concrete harms: (1) Live monitor iterates `for filename in sorted(remote_files)` (bot/services/monitor_tasks_mixin.py:202); filenames are YYYY-MM-DD-HHMMSS-prefixed (regex at line 2152), so lexical sort == chronological, and across cycles only newer files appear. (2) Bulk import sorts: `all_files = sorted(self.stats_dir.glob('*.txt'))` (line 2713) — the 'backfill run' does not process out of order. (3) Rebuild wipes then runs the sorted import. (4) Re-imports are skipped before reaching _create_round_postgresql by is_file_processed (line 1515) and the payload-hash duplicate check (line 1521), so the claimed ON CONFLICT overwrite of a prior-correct gaming_session_id cannot be triggered via re-import. The only residual case — a delayed game-server file arriving later with an older-by->60min timestamp — is theoretically possible but unproven and implausible for sequential round emission. Net: a valid hardening suggestion (add a negative-gap guard), not a proven active corruption bug.
_verify.ground_truth_check: Read _get_or_create_gaming_session_id (lines 2055-2114) and confirmed the global-max query and gap logic exactly as cited. Read _create_round_postgresql (2116-2219): confirmed ON CONFLICT (match_id, round_number) DO UPDATE SET gaming_session_id = EXCLUDED.gaming_session_id at line 2185 and the timestamp-prefixed filename regex at line 2152. Read process_file (1489-1608): confirmed is_file_processed skip at line 1515 and hash-duplicate skip at line 1521 occur before round creation. Read import_all_files (2695-2740): confirmed sorted glob at line 2713. Read monitor_tasks_mixin.py:198-217: confirmed sorted(remote_files) iteration in the live SSH poll. Grepped for file-iteration/sort sites across bot/cogs, bot/core, bot/services.

dropped.title: sessions.js and availability.js loaded without ?v= cache-bust while peers have it (stale-module SPA breakage)
dropped.evidence: In app.js the module graph is cache-busted inconsistently: line 24 `import {...} from './proximity.js?v=20260623-aimlock'`, line 55 `'./session-detail.js?v=20260513-v142-cf-bust'`, line 54 sessions2.js with `?v=`, line 13 utils.js with `?v=`. But line 18 `from './sessions.js'` and line 31 `from './availability.js'` have NO version query (also leaderboard.js, matches.js, home.js, tonight.js). ES-module specifiers do not inherit the entry file's `?v=`, so these are fetched at their bare URL. Per the team's own postmortem (memory: incident_2026-06-25_missing_js_modules.md), Cloudflare caches JS for 24h with a fixed key and a single stale/404 ES module silently halts app.js, leaving hard-coded 'Offline' / empty cards. sessions.js (home Last-Session widget + the entire Sessions browser) and availability.js (the 'evening is the product' core surface) are exactly the modules most damaging to serve stale.
dropped.fix_proposal: Adopt one consistent versioning scheme for ALL local ES-module import specifiers in app.js (and nested imports), bumped per release — e.g. append the same `?v=<release>` to every `./*.js` specifier, or generate the import map at build/deploy. At minimum add `?v=` to sessions.js and availability.js to match their peers. Confirm the deploy script (scripts/etl_update.sh / deploy_release.sh) rewrites every specifier, not just app.js.

_verify.refutation: The finding claims sessions.js/availability.js ship without cache-busting while "peers have it," risking stale-module SPA breakage. This is refuted by the deploy pipeline. scripts/deploy_release.sh step 3b (lines 299-308) auto-injects ?v=$SHA into EVERY bare local import/src specifier across all website/js/*.js and index.html at deploy time. Lines 303-306 run: (1) sed bumping any existing ?v= to ?v=$SHA, and (2) sed injecting ?v=$SHA into bare specifiers via regexes that explicitly cover `from './x.js'`, `from "./x.js"`, `import './x.js'`, `import "./x.js"`. So `from './sessions.js'` and `from './availability.js'` both receive ?v=$SHA on every release. The committed-source inconsistency is cosmetic (only ~10 of 32 imports carry a manual ?v= from recent dev; the OTHER 22 also lack one — so the "peers have it" framing is inaccurate, the majority lack it). The deployed artifact is uniformly versioned. The referenced 2026-06-25 incident was a PARTIAL deploy with 4 missing files (404s), not stale unversioned modules; its remediation was to use clean deploy_release.sh — the exact script that already implements the proposed fix.
_verify.ground_truth_check: Read website/js/app.js lines 1-70 (confirmed line 18 sessions.js and line 31 availability.js have no ?v=, and ~22 of 32 local imports lack ?v=). Confirmed index.html line 4889 loads app.js?v=20260623-aimlock. Inspected scripts/deploy_release.sh lines 242-308: step 3b sed commands inject ?v=$SHA into all website/js/*.js bare imports + index.html src refs every release. etl_update.sh is the game-server updater (unrelated to web JS).

The separate browse view loadSessionsView/loadSessions never uses this widget. On the backend, session-leaderboard with no session_id falls back to get_latest_session_date() (sessions_router.py:371-377), returning the LATEST session leaderboard, not a global/all-time aggregate as the title claims. dropped.title: normalizeMapKey() has the same name but different semantics in proximity.js vs the session modules
dropped.evidence: proximity.js:74 defines `normalizeMapKey(m){ return stripEtColors(m||'').trim().toLowerCase(); }` — only lowercase/trim. sessions.js:58, session-detail.js:77, and sessions2.js:56 define a same-named function that ALSO strips `maps/` prefixes, `.bsp/.pk3/.arena` suffixes, and slugifies non-alphanumerics to underscores. Two functions with an identical name but materially different output is a maintenance trap: any developer (or future shared-helper extraction) who assumes they are interchangeable will introduce a map-keying mismatch bug.
dropped.fix_proposal: Rename proximity.js's variant to reflect its narrower contract (e.g. normalizeProxMapName) or, preferably, consolidate to the canonical slug version in the shared maps.js module and have proximity call it explicitly. At minimum add a one-line comment documenting why proximity needs different behavior.
dropped.why: Same-name/different-behavior helpers are exactly what makes a refactor that 'should be safe' break in production.

_verify.refutation: The textual observation is accurate (proximity.js:74 is lowercase/trim only; sessions.js:58, session-detail.js:77, sessions2.js:56 slugify), but the claimed hazard is refuted. All four files are ES modules imported in website/js/app.js (lines 18/24/54/55), and normalizeMapKey is NOT exported from any of them. It is therefore strictly module-private in each file — no global shadowing, no runtime collision, and no way to accidentally use the wrong one. The 'refactor that should be safe will break' scenario cannot occur via the module system; it would require a developer to deliberately copy/export code. Additionally, the proposed fix points to a 'shared maps.js module' that does not exist in website/js/. The remaining concern is a purely cosmetic same-name nit, far below medium severity, and forced consolidation runs against the project's anti-overengineering north star.
_verify.ground_truth_check: Read proximity.js:74-76 (stripEtColors+trim+lowercase) and sessions.js:58-70 (full slugify). Confirmed identical pattern in session-detail.js and sessions2.js via grep. Confirmed website/js/maps.js does not exist. Confirmed index.html loads only app.js/inline-actions.js/diagnostics.js/lucide-init.js as script tags; the proximity/session files load via module imports, each with its own scope.
